question_title: Will US existing home sales (seasonally adjusted annual rate) for March 2026 be at least 4.0 million?
summary: March 2026 existing home sales have been in a stable regime around 4.06M SAAR for 13 months, with 84.6% of months exceeding 4.0M. The January 2026 dip to 3.91M was explicitly weather-driven per NAR, and the only prior sub-4.0M month (June 2025 at 3.98M) saw immediate recovery. With 2 months to recover, spring seasonal tailwinds, mortgage rates declining to ~6.0%, and weekly pending sales showing YoY recovery, the odds favor crossing the 4.0M threshold. Modest headwinds from tariff uncertainty and weak (but weather-affected) January pending sales provide some downside risk. Monte Carlo models weighted toward mean-reversion give ~64%, with qualitative spring/rate boost to ~67%.

factors.description: Regime stability: 11/13 months (84.6%) were >= 4.0M SAAR. Regime mean 4.063M, std 0.084M. Under N(4.063, 0.084), P(X>=4.0) ≈ 77%. Threshold is below regime mean.
January 2026 weather-driven dip explicitly confirmed by NAR. After only other sub-4.0M month (Jun 2025 at 3.98M), immediate recovery to 4.03M in July. Two-month recovery window.
Spring seasonal + mortgage rates: March is spring buying season (March 2025: 4.02M). Mortgage rates at ~6.0%, best level in months. Affordability best since March 2022.
Weekly recovery signals: Weekly pending sales returned to YoY growth in February, purchase mortgage applications +8% YoY through all 6 weeks of 2026.
Weak January pending sales (-0.8% MoM) occurred during same weather-affected month. Partially double-counts weather dip.
Tariff/economic uncertainty: Real but speculative in magnitude for existing home sales (contracts signed 30-60 days prior).

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.67 (67% probability YES)**

Key reasoning:
- The existing home sales regime over the past 13 months is centered at 4.063M with 84.6% of months above the 4.0M threshold
- January 2026's dip to 3.91M was explicitly weather-driven per NAR, and historical precedent shows quick recovery from sub-4.0M months
- Spring seasonal effects, declining mortgage rates (~6.0%), and weekly pending sales recovery all support a return above 4.0M
- Modest headwinds from tariff uncertainty and weak pending sales (themselves weather-affected) discount the probability from the ~77% regime base rate down to ~67%
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will US existing home sales (seasonally adjusted annual rate) for March 2026 be at least 4.0 million?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42499)
[Weekly pending home sales back to year-over-year growth as we head into spring](https://www.housingwire.com/articles/pending-sales-inventory-growth-2026/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the National Association of Realtors reports March 2026 existing home sales at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.0 million or higher in its first publication reporting March 2026, released before May 1, 2026. This question resolves to no if the first reported March 2026 SAAR is below 4.0 million. The value will be taken from NAR’s Existing-Home Sales page at https://www.nar.realtor/research-and-statistics/housing-statistics/existing-home-sales
fine_print: Use the first reported March 2026 SAAR (ignore later revisions). Use the headline “Existing-home sales” SAAR level. Candidate Sources: NAR press release linked from the Existing-Home Sales page (backup) https://www.nar.realtor/newsroom | FRED series EXHOSLUSM495S (for cross-check only) https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/EXHOSLUSM495S
background_info:
This forecasts housing-market transaction volume, a key channel for consumption, credit demand, and construction-related activity.
